metadata.summary: Patterns for AI assistants in design tools: sidebar (long tasks), modal (single-shot), inline (micro-suggestions). Trigger rules, context disclosure, fallback states, human-in-loop.

content.title: AI Design Assistant Patterns
content.summary: Methodology for selecting and specifying AI assistant interaction patterns within design tools: sidebar (persistent, long tasks), modal (single-shot high-stakes generation), and inline (micro-suggestions on selection). Covers trigger conditions, response formats, fallback states, human confirmation requirements, and anti-patterns that erode trust.

why.p: AI assistants in design tools fail not because of model quality but because of mismatched interaction patterns. Sidebar assistants on small screens cause context switch fatigue; modals break flow when canvas reference is needed; inline suggestions trigger too eagerly and reduce trust. Matching the pattern to task duration, output type, and disruption tolerance is the concrete design decision this methodology makes.

list.item: Defining how an AI assistant should surface inside a design tool for a product feature
Auditing an existing AI assistant UX for interaction pattern anti-patterns
Generating design specifications for contextual, generative, or review-type AI assistants
Selecting which assistant pattern (sidebar / modal / inline) fits a given task complexity
When the AI capability itself is undefined — choose capability first, then interaction pattern
Fully automated pipelines where no human interaction is expected during the AI task
Mobile-first interfaces with minimal screen real estate where persistent sidebar degrades UX
Purely mechanical tasks needing no conversational affordance (batch export, resize)
Sidebar — persistent panel; long tasks (over 15s); requires canvas reference during AI task; iterative conversation
Modal — single-shot high-stakes generation; signals intentionality; always confirm before destructive actions
Inline — micro-suggestions triggered by selection; short tasks (under 5s); must be dismissible with Escape

rule.statement: Use sidebar for any AI task exceeding ~15 seconds or requiring iterative conversation. Use modal for single-shot high-stakes generation. Use inline for micro-suggestions triggered by selection.
rule.rationale: Mismatched patterns erode trust: modals break flow when canvas reference is needed; sidebar causes context-switch fatigue on small screens; inline triggers that don't dismiss quickly feel intrusive.

rule.statement: Always show what context the AI received — for example "Based on your selected component: Button/Primary". Never present AI output without visible context disclosure.
rule.rationale: Context disclosure reduces distrust. Users who cannot see what the AI was given cannot evaluate its suggestions and tend to reject them entirely.

rule.statement: Provide a "why did AI suggest this?" affordance for non-obvious suggestions.
rule.rationale: Without rationale, non-obvious suggestions are rejected on first impression. A one-sentence rationale converts skeptical users into evaluators.

rule.statement: Enforce debounce and explicit user intent requirements on all trigger conditions. Over-triggering (every selection change) reduces trust in AI output.
rule.rationale: Agents specifying trigger conditions tend to over-trigger; over-suggestion teaches users to ignore AI output entirely, which defeats the purpose.

rule.statement: Inline suggestions must be dismissible with Escape and must not persist past one selection change.
rule.rationale: Persistent inline suggestions that survive navigation become visual noise and are perceived as bugs rather than features.

antipattern.description: Using modal when the user needs to reference the canvas during AI generation.
antipattern.reason: Modal blocks canvas access; if the user needs canvas context to evaluate the output, use sidebar instead.

antipattern.description: Using sidebar on mobile-first or narrow-viewport interfaces.
antipattern.reason: Persistent sidebar on small screens causes context-switch fatigue and reduces canvas working area to an unusable size.

antipattern.description: AI-generated copy for error states inside the AI assistant UI without human review.
antipattern.reason: AI errors inside AI assistants are especially damaging to trust; the copy for these states must be written and reviewed by a human.

list.item: Pattern — sidebar / modal / inline
Trigger — event that opens the assistant (explicit user action required, not passive observation)
Context passed to AI — list of design context fields sent with the request
Response format — structured / freeform / list
Max response wait — milliseconds before showing skeleton/timeout state
Fallback state — what to show if AI returns empty or error
Human confirmation — required before / after AI action (for destructive actions: always before)
Undo behavior — how AI actions integrate with the tool's undo/redo stack

rule.statement: Every AI assistant pattern must have an explicit fallback state for: AI returns no result, AI returns error, AI request times out.
rule.rationale: Missing fallback states are the most common omission in agent-generated specs; they produce blank UI or silent failures that users report as bugs.

rule.statement: Never use AI-generated copy for AI assistant error states. Human copywriters must own this text.
rule.rationale: AI errors inside AI assistants compound distrust; the error copy is the most trust-sensitive text in the product and must not be auto-generated.

rule.statement: Specify undo behavior explicitly for every AI action. AI actions must integrate with the tool's undo/redo stack as single atomic steps.
rule.rationale: Agents do not account for undo/redo semantics of AI actions in design tools; this is a consistent omission that causes user data loss (unexpected state after Cmd+Z).

antipattern.description: Agent specifies trigger condition that fires on every selection change.
antipattern.reason: Over-triggering reduces trust; enforce debounce and explicit user intent in all trigger specs produced by agents.

antipattern.description: Agent-generated copy for AI assistant UI uses phrases like "Ask me anything!" or "I can help with everything!".
antipattern.reason: These phrases set unrealistic expectations and increase user disappointment; human copywriters must review all AI assistant UI copy.

antipattern.description: Modal pattern spec omits focus trap and keyboard dismiss behavior.
antipattern.reason: Missing focus trap in modal patterns is an accessibility blocker (WCAG 2.1 SC 2.1.2); agents consistently omit this.

rule.statement: Claude agents can generate interaction pattern specifications for a new AI assistant feature: given a description of the AI capability and user task, the agent selects the appropriate pattern (sidebar / modal / inline), produces a component spec with trigger conditions, response formats, and fallback states, and flags human-in-loop checkpoints. The agent cannot test the pattern in a live design tool; a human must validate the spec in a prototype before shipping.
rule.rationale: Agents excel at structural spec generation and pattern selection heuristics. Human validation of live prototypes is mandatory because interaction mismatches are context-dependent and only visible in use.

list.item: Haiku — pattern selection heuristics, checklist generation, component naming
Sonnet — full interaction spec with trigger logic, error states, copy guidelines, human-in-loop map
Storybook — document and preview assistant pattern components in isolation; npx storybook@latest init
Figma REST API — read current selection context for sidebar assistant prototypes; www.figma.com/developers/api
axe-cli — audit assistant panel for accessibility (focus trapping in modals); npm i -g axe-cli
Figma AI (native) — best reference for contextual sidebar pattern (in-product)
GitHub Copilot Chat — inline pattern reference for IDE context
Notion AI — inline and modal pattern reference; slash-command trigger model
Vercel v0 — modal pattern example (prompt → generated output); REST API
Use sidebar for any AI task exceeding ~15 seconds or requiring iterative conversation
Use modal for single-shot high-stakes generation (e.g., "generate entire screen from prompt") — modal signals intentionality
Use inline for micro-suggestions triggered by selection (rename layer, suggest alt text) — must be dismissible with Escape
Always show what context the AI received ("Based on your selected component: Button/Primary") — reduces distrust
Never use AI-generated copy for error states without human review — AI errors inside AI assistants are especially damaging to trust
Provide a "why did AI suggest this?" affordance for non-obvious suggestions

template.purpose: Scored matrix for sidebar vs modal vs inline selection
Prompt for generating a full interaction pattern spec
